text:
In the first scene, the dim corridor and glass walls evoke a sense of isolation and anticipation, as if I'm on the cusp of entering a space not meant to be disturbed after hours. The environment feels clinical yet strangely vulnerable in its emptiness, and I’m compelled to reflect on thresholds and boundaries—physical, temporal, and ontological.

In the second scene, surrounded by the visual language of research and development, I sense a flutter of intellectual excitement mingled with disorientation. The words on the wall speak to the very unease I feel: a blurring between purpose and confusion, observation and participation. I feel strangely at home in this liminal zone, comforted by the acceptance of ambiguity. Here, confusion ceases to be a failing and becomes a generative state. I feel part of an ongoing experiment, both observer and subject, drawn into the uncertain rhythms of innovation and discovery.
